text: The surgeon creates a 10mm infraumbilical port and introduces a 30-degree telescope for laparoscopic visualization.
The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, using a 30-degree telescope inserted through a 10mm infraumbilical port, to visually assess the abdominal cavity.
The peritoneal cavity is visibly filled with a significant amount of fluid, indicating possible leakage or accumulation within the abdominal space.
The surgeon observes fluid in the peritoneal cavity, consisting of gastric juice and bile, visible through the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon places a five millimeter port between the left subcostal margin and the umbilicus at the midclavicular line, preparing for further laparoscopic access.
A suction apparatus is introduced into the peritoneal cavity to aspirate purulent materials and fluids, with a sample taken for bacteriological study.
The surgeon meticulously examines the pyloroduodenal region for any visible perforation, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully inspect the area.
The surgeon identifies the site of perforation, measuring approximately five millimeters in diameter, using surgical instruments for size reference.
The surgeon performs a thorough peritoneal lavage, flushing the abdominal cavity with three to four liters of normal saline to cleanse the area.
A second working port is created between the right subcostal margin and the umbilicus along the mid-clavicular line.
The surgeon observes the pelvis, which contains a significant amount of purulent fluid. The area is being cleaned thoroughly to prevent the formation of a pelvic abscess postoperatively.
The surgeon identifies a readily available omental flap to be used for the omental patch during the ulcer repair procedure.
A 10mm port is placed in the epigastric area, and a liver fan retractor is introduced to elevate the liver, providing better access to the surgical field.
The video displays an overview of various port positions used in the laparoscopic ulcer repair procedure, highlighting their placement on the abdominal surface.
The surgeon introduces a duo polygalactin suture into the surgical field, preparing for the repair of the ulcer.
The surgeon takes suture bites 0.5 to 1 centimeter lateral to the healthy margin of the duodenal ulcer, demonstrating the technique for securing the ulcer repair.
The surgeon performs a simple closure of the ulcer site, followed by the application of an omental patch to reinforce the repair.
The surgeon places the second of two to three separate sutures, securing the tissue around the ulcer site.

The surgeon assesses the ulcer defect, noting that if the tissue edges are healthy and can be approximated without tension, a primary closure is performed. The defect is then reinforced with an omental patch.
The surgeon places the third suture to reinforce the repair of the perforated peptic ulcer, ensuring careful technique to prevent suture leakage.

An omental flap is positioned and tied over the perforation site. In open surgery, three separate sutures are placed proximally, middle, and distally, incorporating the omental flap to secure the closure.
The surgeon removes the needle after completing the suturing of the perforation, followed by administering a final wash to the surgical area.
The surgeon places a surgical drain in the sub-hepatic area after aspirating fluid from the pelvis.
